blurb: A tidy chartreuse dome that blazes red-orange in autumn.
seasons: Sulfur-yellow bracts spring; green summer; red/orange/purple fall
toxic: Milky sap irritates skin/eyes — wear gloves; caution with kids/pets
origin: Central and SE Europe to NW Turkey
habitat: Rocky open hillsides, dry woodland edges, and scrub on limestone

care.hardiness: USDA zones 4–8 — fully winter-hardy on the Boulder Front Range (zone 5b–6a). The mound dies back to the crown in winter and re-emerges reliably each spring. No protection needed beyond good drainage; sitting in wet soil through freeze-thaw cycles is the one thing that kills it.[1][3]
care.sun: Full sun is ideal — 6+ hours gives the tightest, brightest dome. It tolerates light afternoon shade, but too much shade causes the plant to lose its characteristic cushion shape and become open and floppy.[1]
care.soil: Thrives in lean, dry to medium, sharply drained soil — tolerates poor, rocky, sandy, or clay ground as long as it doesn't stay wet. The alkaline clay-loam soils common on the Front Range suit it well without amendment. Rich, heavily amended, or constantly moist soil leads to lax growth and dulls fall color.[1]
care.water: Low and drought-tolerant once established — it gets by on Front Range rainfall (~15–18 in/year) with only supplemental watering during extended dry spells. Water deeply but infrequently through the growing season; avoid overhead irrigation that keeps foliage wet, and ease off entirely in late fall to help harden the crown before freeze-up.[1]
care.spacing: Space 12–18 in apart; each plant matures to roughly 12–18 in tall and as wide (occasionally wider in rich soil). The compact dome habit means it rarely needs adjustment — set it and leave it.[1]
care.bloom: Showy sulfur-yellow bracts appear in April–May on the Front Range, typically 3–4 weeks of peak color. The lime-chartreuse display is long-lasting even after the true flowers fade. Foliage turns vivid red-orange-purple in autumn for a second season of interest.[1]
care.feeding: None needed on average to poor soils — lean conditions keep the mound tight and bright. A light balanced fertilizer in early spring is fine on genuinely poor ground, but skip it on typical Front Range soils; excess nutrients produce loose, soft growth and muted fall color.[1]
care.maintenance: Cut spent flowering shoots back to the base in late summer or fall once color fades — this keeps the mound tidy and prevents unwanted self-seeding. Wear gloves (and eye protection when pruning): the milky white latex sap is a significant skin and mucous-membrane irritant. Do not compost prunings carelessly — wash tools afterward. Divide clumps in early spring every 4–5 years if the center begins to open up; take basal cuttings in spring, dipping cut ends in water to stop sap flow before potting.[1][4]
care.selfsow: Can self-seed modestly; deadhead promptly after bloom if you want to control spread. Seedlings are easy to spot and pull when small.[1]
care.troubles: Remarkably trouble-free — no serious pests or diseases on the Front Range. The one vulnerability is crown rot from poorly drained or chronically wet soil; ensure good drainage, especially over winter. Sap causes contact dermatitis in sensitive people and is harmful to eyes — keep kids and pets away from cut stems.[1][4]
care.companions: Pairs beautifully with late-spring bloomers that share its lean, well-drained conditions: blue flax, bearded iris, wallflower, catmint, and ornamental alliums. The bright spring bracts contrast well with blue-purple tones. Fall foliage color reads well next to little bluestem grass.[1]
care.planting: Plant container-grown specimens in spring (April to mid-May) or in early fall (September to early October) on the Front Range — both windows give roots time to settle before the harshest conditions. Spring is most common; fall works well because cooler soil temperatures reduce transplant stress. Good drainage at planting is non-negotiable: if your site holds any standing water, raise the bed or amend with coarse grit before setting in the crown. In containers, cushion spurge can overwinter outdoors in a large pot (at least 12–14 in.) with a lean, gritty mix and a sheltered spot, but the main risk is the wet-winter crown rot the species is prone to — use a pot with generous drainage holes, elevate it off the ground, and protect from prolonged soggy conditions. Small or poorly drained containers are risky; bring them under cover in late fall.[1]
care.propagation: Division in early spring — just as new growth emerges from the crown — is the easiest and most reliable method; dig the clump, split it into sections each with several crowns and roots, and replant immediately at the same depth. Divide every 4–5 years if the center becomes open or unproductive. Basal cuttings taken in spring through early summer (new shoots 3–4 in. long) also root well: sever close to the base, immediately dip the cut end in lukewarm water or powdered charcoal to stop sap bleed, then insert in a gritty rooting mix. Wear gloves throughout — the milky latex is a skin and eye irritant. Seed is possible but slow: surface-sow on gritty compost in autumn and leave outside for winter cold stratification; germination is erratic. For most gardeners, spring division is the go-to approach.[1][2]

edible.summary: Cushion spurge is poisonous — all parts are toxic if eaten and its milky sap is a skin and eye irritant. No part is edible.[3][4]
edible.caution: Like all Euphorbia, cushion spurge bleeds a toxic milky-white latex (the irritant compounds are diterpene esters) whenever a part is cut or broken. All parts — flowers, fruit, leaves, stems, and sap — are toxic if ingested, causing burning of the lips, mouth, and throat and, in worse cases, vomiting and diarrhea. Skin contact with the sap can cause redness, swelling, and blistering; eye contact is especially dangerous, causing redness, blurred vision, and potentially lasting corneal damage. Keep it away from children and pets and never put any part in the mouth.[3][4]
